questions.q: First-past-the-post (FPTP) electoral systems are characterized by
options: Each voter ranks candidates in order of preference
The candidate receiving the most votes in a district wins, regardless of whether they achieve a majority
All votes are weighted equally according to population density
Multiple rounds of voting until a candidate achieves 50% plus one votes
questions.explanation: FPTP awards the seat to the highest vote-getter in a district without requiring a majority. It is simple but can result in winners with well under 50% of votes when three or more candidates compete. Ranked choice and runoff systems operate differently.

questions.q: Proportional representation electoral systems aim to
options: Ensure the largest party always controls a legislature
Mirror parties' vote share with seat share in the legislature
Give all seats to the party winning the most votes in any region
Eliminate the influence of smaller political parties
questions.explanation: Proportional representation ties seats to vote totals—a party winning 30% of votes receives approximately 30% of seats. This contrasts with FPTP, which can give a party with 40% of votes a legislative supermajority. PR encourages coalition governments.

options: Fraud in vote counting or ballot access restrictions
The manipulation of electoral district boundaries to favor a particular party
The use of exit polls to predict election outcomes
The requirement that voters present identification at polling places
questions.explanation: Gerrymandering is the deliberate redrawing of district boundaries to concentrate supporters of one party (packing) or dilute them (cracking). While vote restrictions and identity requirements are electoral issues, they are distinct from boundary manipulation.

options: Universal satisfaction with all available candidates and parties
Disengagement from politics, lack of faith in democracy, or barriers to voting access
That fewer votes are required for a winning candidate to be elected
That the electorate has achieved consensus on all major issues
questions.explanation: Low turnout typically reflects apathy, distrust of political institutions, or voting obstacles—not satisfaction or consensus. Higher turnout is generally valued as reflecting broader representation of the electorate.

questions.q: The majority vote requirement in some electoral systems (such as runoff elections) exists to ensure
options: Only the candidate with the most votes wins
A winning candidate has explicit support from more than 50% of voters, increasing perceived legitimacy
Smaller parties cannot participate in elections
Campaign spending is equally distributed
questions.explanation: Majority requirements (via runoffs, instant runoff, or other mechanisms) ensure winners have explicit support from over half of voters, increasing democratic legitimacy. Plurality winners with 35% of votes in three-way races might lack this legitimacy in the eyes of many voters.

questions.q: Campaign finance regulations in democracies typically attempt to
options: Ensure the richest candidates always win
Limit the influence of wealthy individuals or corporations on electoral outcomes
Eliminate all political spending by special interest groups
Guarantee that candidates with less money win elections
questions.explanation: Finance regulations seek to prevent wealth from overwhelming electoral fairness—via contribution limits, disclosure, or public financing. They do not guarantee poor candidates win, eliminate all group spending (which may violate free speech), or favor the wealthy.

questions.q: Which of the following is a potential disadvantage of proportional representation systems?
options: They guarantee every party wins seats equal to vote share
They often necessitate coalition governments that may be unstable
They are simpler for voters to understand than FPTP systems
They prevent the formation of minority governments
questions.explanation: PR often produces fragmented parliaments requiring coalitions, which can be unstable or slow to form. Majority-producing systems like FPTP may create less stable outcomes than weak coalitions. PR's seat-to-vote alignment is an advantage, not disadvantage.

questions.q: Early voting and mail-in voting mechanisms primarily serve to
options: Reduce election security and increase fraud risk
Increase voter convenience and access, particularly for those with scheduling conflicts or mobility issues
Eliminate the need for polling places entirely
Decrease overall voter turnout by making voting seem less important
questions.explanation: Early and mail voting reduce barriers for working people, caregivers, travelers, and disabled voters. Extensive evidence suggests early and mail voting does not significantly increase fraud, though security measures remain important. These options increase convenience without reducing participation.

questions.q: The concept of electoral competitiveness refers to
options: The total number of candidates running for office
Whether elections are close and uncertain, with multiple viable contenders
The cost and complexity of organizing elections
The literacy level required to cast ballots
questions.explanation: Competitive elections feature multiple candidates with genuine chances of winning, creating uncertainty and motivation for campaigns. Non-competitive or predetermined elections occur when one party or candidate so dominates that the outcome is never in doubt.

questions.q: Voter suppression tactics historically and in modern contexts include
options: Providing voter education materials in multiple languages
Restricting voting hours, locations, or methods, and aggressive voter ID requirements that disproportionately affect minorities
Public campaigns encouraging all eligible citizens to register and vote
Increasing accessibility for voters with disabilities or language barriers
questions.explanation: Suppression involves making voting harder through timing, location, documentation, or identification barriers that fall disproportionately on marginalized groups. Education, accessibility, and registration drives increase participation, not suppress it.
